![View profile for Nick Bradley 🌱🥩]()

Reposting this from [Adam Bergman](https://www.linkedin.com/in/adam-e-bergman?trk=public_post-text) at [EcoTech Capital](https://www.linkedin.com/company/ecotech-capital?trk=public_post-text) as I’m here in Chicago for [The Future of Protein Production](https://nl.linkedin.com/company/the-future-of-protein-production?trk=public_post-text) 🇺🇸 Haven’t managed to find Adam on the ground yet to say hello – but the themes he outlines here are very much part of the conversation this week.
Longer timelines. Tougher capital markets. More realism around what food tech is – and what it isn’t.
It feels like the sector is in a more disciplined phase now. Less exuberance, more execution.
Looking forward to catching up in person before the end of tomorrow!

🇫🇷 French foodtech firm [Verley](https://fr.linkedin.com/company/verleyfood?trk=public_post-text) has completed a €32m Series A funding round to accelerate the commercial launch of its functional dairy alternatives.
Led by [Alven](https://fr.linkedin.com/company/alven-vc?trk=public_post-text), the investment supports the company’s mission to supply high-quality protein ingredients while reducing the environmental footprint of traditional dairy production.
The startup specialises in producing beta-lactoglobulin through precision fermentation, a process designed to integrate directly into existing industrial food supply chains.
With this fresh capital, the business plans to enter the US market and expand its production capacity to meet the rising global demand for clean-label nutritional products.
🚀 Verley has developed the FermWhey™ range to provide enhanced solubility and gelling properties for high-protein yoghurts and shakes.
🚀 The company recently secured a "No Questions" letter from the FDA, clearing the path for its strategic expansion across North America.
🚀 Chief Executive and co-founder [Stéphane Mac Millan](https://fr.linkedin.com/in/st%C3%A9phane-mac-millan-1b4a3016?trk=public_post-text) is positioning the firm as a European champion within the evolving dairy landscape.
🚀 New backers including Blast and the French Tech Seed fund joined historical investors [Sofinnova Partners](https://fr.linkedin.com/company/sofinnova-partners?trk=public_post-text), [Sparkfood](https://pt.linkedin.com/company/sparkfood?trk=public_post-text), Captech, and [Founders Future](https://fr.linkedin.com/company/founders-future?trk=public_post-text).
It is brilliant to see Stéphane Mac Millan and the Verley team bridging the gap between biotechnology and mainstream nutrition.
--

results.content: Excited to announce that Marcus Schindler just joined Cradle's advisory board. Marcus was Chief Scientific Officer at Novo Nordisk,

![View profile for Stef van Grieken]()

Excited to announce that Marcus Schindler just joined [Cradle](https://nl.linkedin.com/company/cradlebio?trk=public_post-text)'s advisory board.
Marcus was Chief Scientific Officer at [Novo Nordisk](https://dk.linkedin.com/company/novo-nordisk?trk=public_post-text), where he oversaw R&D across multiple therapeutic areas and modalities. But what makes him special is that he's one of the few pharma leaders who's actually made AI adoption work at scale.
That matters because the hardest problem in AI for drug discovery isn't building a better model, but getting an organization of scientists who spends $80K–$1M per experimental round to rely on the fact that what you're generating will actually succeed in the wet lab.
We've all read that 95% of AI pilots deliver little P&L impact. The core reason for that is not that the technology doesn't work, but that adoption is a change management problem.
Marcus understands what it takes to make that happen inside the world's largest pharma organizations. That's exactly the experience we need as Cradle now powers 50+ R&D programs across 6 of the top 25 global biopharma companies, way beyond just pilot projects.
Welcome to the team, Marcus.

## More Relevant Posts

![View profile for Yiannis Kiachopoulos]()

I’m very pleased to welcome [Marcus Schindler](https://se.linkedin.com/in/marcus-schindler-phd?trk=public_post-text) to [Causaly](https://uk.linkedin.com/company/causaly?trk=public_post-text)'s Strategic Advisory Board.
 
Transforming drug discovery with AI requires an understanding of how R&D organizations operate at scale, how portfolios are shaped, and how scientific decisions translate into impact for patients. These realities are at the heart of our product, supported by our in-house PhD scientists and a Strategic Advisory Board with expertise at the highest levels of R&D.
 
With a long successful track record in the pharmaceutical industry, most recently as Chief Scientific Officer and EVP of Research & Early Development at Novo Nordisk, Marcus has led large, global organizations and extensive programme portfolios, while modernizing R&D into data- and AI-enabled organizations.
His decades shaping the next generation of drug discovery will bring valuable perspective as we continue advancing the Causaly platform.
 
I'm excited to collaborate with Marcus in pushing the boundaries for the use of AI in life sciences, and ultimately accelerating delivery of new therapies to patients.

In biotech, a single execution failure can quietly erase $5M–$20M in valuation.
Not because the science fails.
Because alignment fails.
AI-driven drug discovery platforms are generating therapeutic signals faster than ever.
But across many biotech organizations, strategy, portfolio priorities, and execution teams drift out of alignment.
When that happens, even promising programs stall long before reaching clinical validation.
Tomorrow morning I’ll share a short perspective on the alignment gap in biotech — and why strategy execution and portfolio governance may become the real competitive advantage in AI-driven drug development.
Subscribe → [https://lnkd.in/eHFjf-eN](https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=https%3A%2F%2Flnkd%2Ein%2FeHFjf-eN&urlhash=coNr&trk=public_post-text)

Last Thursday (March 5), I had the opportunity to attend the [Festival of Biologics](https://www.linkedin.com/company/festivalofbiologics?trk=public_post-text) USA 2026 in San Diego. It was my first large conference in the United States, and it was inspiring to be surrounded by so many professionals working across the biologics and biotech space.
During several panels, one theme kept returning: the growing role of AI in the future of biotechnology. Many speakers highlighted how artificial intelligence is becoming essential for accelerating research, improving efficiency, and even facilitating communication with regulatory agencies like the U.S. Food and Drug Administration.
One example that stood out came from Weave Bio, which shared how integrating AI into a partner company’s workflow reduced hands-on time by 50%, accelerated IND submissions by 66%, and sped up NDA timelines by 60%. Experiences like this make it clear that AI is not just a trend but a tool that will increasingly shape how we work in biotech.
Speakers from the Mayo Clinic also discussed the role of AI in the future of immunotherapy, emphasizing its potential to accelerate research and help deliver the right therapy to the right patient at the right time.
Beyond the panels, one of the most valuable parts of the conference was connecting with professionals from many different companies. Some advice I heard repeatedly stayed with me:
• Wherever you work, the relationships you build are the names and people you carry with you throughout your career.
• Be a cross-learner and stay curious about fields beyond your own.
• Say yes to opportunities. Being open often leads to unexpected growth.
• Your process and the story behind your journey matter.
Grateful for the chance to learn, listen, and meet so many inspiring people in the field.

I see deal sourcing entering a new phase. As Ranu and Tyler point out, a lot of future value is sitting in earlier-stage or under-scaled assets that generate limited revenue today but hold meaningful growth potential.
AI-powered always-on deal radar brings real-time intelligence into the process. Instead of periodic outreach, teams can maintain constant visibility, identify opportunities earlier, and evaluate them with greater confidence.
From a transactions perspective, pipeline renewal pressures and competition keep rising. I believe moving from episodic sourcing to persistent insight could shape the next chapter of M&A execution.

![View profile for Ranu Nandy Carroll]()

When I first started in biopharma dealmaking, finding the right acquisition or licensing targets often felt like looking for a needle in a haystack. Today, the landscape is bigger than ever, and the most promising candidates are often small or precommercial biotech companies—companies that now account for a tiny fraction of revenue but could drive a huge share of future growth.
Tyler Charlesworth and I wrote this article to explore a new approach: AI-powered “always-on” deal radars. These systems scan everything from scientific papers to trial data, helping teams spot opportunities early and focus on what really matters.
If you’re curious about how AI can help biopharma companies spot opportunities earlier and strengthen their pipelines, check out the full piece below:
